findings.title: onAnyMessage test never verifies message delivery
findings.code_snippet:
const messages: IncomingMessage[] = []
registry.onAnyMessage((msg) => { messages.push(msg) })

expect(messages).toHaveLength(0)
findings.description: The test for onAnyMessage only verifies the handler is subscribed and the array starts empty. It never triggers a message through any registered channel to verify that the global handler actually receives messages. This is a critical behavioral gap -- the core purpose of onAnyMessage (fan-in from multiple channels) is untested. The ChannelRegistry.register() method wires channel.onMessage to forward to globalHandlers, but no test proves this wiring works.

findings.title: SlackChannel and WhatsAppChannel connect-without-deps error untested
findings.description: Both SlackChannel.connect() (line 64-71) and WhatsAppChannel.connect() (line 73-75) throw errors when called without injected dependencies, but no test verifies these error paths. The tests for 'should throw when sending before connect' only test the sendMessage-before-connect case, not the connect-without-deps case. These are distinct error paths -- one is a state guard, the other is a dependency configuration error.

findings.title: disconnectAll fault isolation not tested
findings.code_snippet:
it('should disconnect all registered channels', async () => {
  ...
  await registry.connectAll()
  await registry.disconnectAll()
  expect(wa.status).toBe('disconnected')
  expect(slack.status).toBe('disconnected')
})
findings.description: The connectAll method has a fault isolation test (line 95-111) that verifies one channel failing does not block others. The disconnectAll method (channel-registry.ts line 62-80) has identical Promise.allSettled-style fault isolation logic, but no corresponding test. If one channel throws during disconnect, the test suite does not verify that other channels are still properly disconnected.

findings.title: WhatsApp extendedTextMessage path untested
findings.description: The WhatsApp message handler supports two text extraction paths: conversation (direct text) and extendedTextMessage.text (forwarded/quoted messages). All tests only use the conversation path. The extendedTextMessage fallback is untested -- if the optional chaining or nullish coalescing has a bug, tests will not catch it.

findings.title: WhatsApp message with no text content not tested
findings.code_snippet:
if (!msg.message) continue

const text = msg.message.conversation
  ?? msg.message.extendedTextMessage?.text
  ?? ''

if (!text) continue
findings.description: The WhatsApp handler has guards for messages with no message property and messages where text resolves to empty string. Neither boundary condition is tested. A message with { message: {} } (no conversation, no extendedTextMessage) should be silently skipped -- this is an important edge case for media-only messages (images, stickers, etc.) that the WhatsApp protocol delivers.

findings.title: Slack message with empty text not tested
findings.description: The Slack message handler guards against messages with empty or undefined text (line 112), but no test covers this boundary condition. Slack sends events for message edits, deletions, and subtype messages that may have no text field. Without a test, a regression that removes this guard would silently pass invalid messages to handlers.

findings.title: ChannelStatus 'error' state never tested or transitioned to
findings.description: The ChannelStatus type includes an 'error' state, but neither SlackChannel nor WhatsAppChannel ever transitions to 'error' status. When connect() fails, the status is left in 'connecting' state (the error is thrown before setStatus('connected') is called, and no catch block sets 'error'). No test verifies what status the channel is in after a failed connection attempt. This is either a missing implementation or a dead type variant -- either way it should be tested to surface the issue.

positive_observations: Both SlackChannel and WhatsAppChannel test files include proper '// Test Double rationale:' comments explaining why external API mocking is necessary
Test doubles for external APIs (@slack/bolt, @whiskeysockets/baileys) use dependency injection via constructor, making the test double boundary explicit and swappable
WhatsApp rate limiting is tested with a concrete scenario proving the boundary enforcement
Channel registry tests cover the full CRUD lifecycle: register, retrieve, list, unregister, and duplicate prevention
Both channel implementations test message handler unsubscription, ensuring no memory leaks from lingering handlers
Fault isolation for connectAll is properly tested, verifying one channel failure does not cascade to others
